videos.title: Setting Up Tailscale for My Remote Agent
videos.description:
I tried running Hermes Desktop as a proactive 24/7 AI agent on my MacBook Air while I was traveling.

The setup worked at first, but I made one big mistake: the only way I had to talk to the agent remotely was through Discord. A day or two into the trip, that channel broke, and I had no way to connect back to the machine running the agent.

This is why I’m setting up Tailscale as a secure remote access layer for my local AI agent setup.

If you’re experimenting with always-on agents, local AI tools, self-hosted workflows, or desktop agents like Hermes, remote access and fallback infrastructure matter way more than you think.

videos.title: I’m Building 24/7 AI Agents For My Real Life
videos.description:
First library talk. I’m testing a simpler talking-head format and unpacking where my channel is going: AI coding as a niche, the current economics of inference, why subsidized plans like Codex Pro are such a big opportunity, and what I actually want 24/7 agents to do in my life.

I also talk through two concrete agent goals I’m thinking about right now: a health assistant and a content creation system, plus what I learned from trying to run Hermes Agent on a dedicated laptop.

videos.title: Hermes Desktop Actually Works Better Than I Expected
videos.description:
I spent over eight months building my own desktop agent workflow. Then I tried Hermes Desktop live, and within the first session I was seriously considering switching.

This is my first real test of Hermes Agent Desktop from Nous Research. I compare it against the agent workflow I’ve been building, test the desktop UI, voice conversation, text-to-speech, speech-to-text, memory/context settings, skills, profiles, and the ability to create a new AJ profile with my existing agent setup.

The wild part: Hermes didn’t just feel polished. It hit the exact layer I care about most, which is persistent agent context, profiles, skills, and long-running workflows that can actually help move goals forward.

It was not flawless. I hit a couple bugs, including missing skills and some trust-friction around credentials. But for a live first try, this was much stronger than I expected.

Original Stream: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lEOTCZgxdho
Hermes Agent: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs/

videos.description:
Pi AutoResearch is a compact example of harness engineering: instead of manually guessing, you set up a feedback loop that tries experiments, measures results, keeps the winners, and discards the regressions.

In this clip, I break down why pi-autoresearch is interesting, how it came out of Shopify’s work, what “autoresearch” actually means, and how to install and run it in Pi.

If you care about AI agents, coding harnesses, optimization loops, or practical ways to make agent workflows improve themselves, this is the core idea.

videos.title: The First AI Coding Cup Had a Chess Engine Meta
videos.description:
I ran the first Vibe Code Cup, a live AI-allowed coding competition where builders had one timed window, one final submission, and a concrete objective challenge: build a standalone chess agent that takes a FEN/current board state and returns legal moves.

This condensed cut explains the format, the rules, the judging harness, and the real lesson from the event: the strongest AI coding workflow was about research, validation, tournament simulation, and fast feedback loops. The winning agent, Deeper Blue, came from adapting an existing JavaScript chess engine and validating it under the contest constraints, while other competitors explored multi-agent factories, internal tournaments, and from-scratch approaches.

If you care about AI coding, agentic software workflows, coding competitions, chess engines, or how to design objective AI-native events, this is the full story from Vibe Code Cup #1.

Vibe Code Cup: https://vibe-code-cup.com/
Original livestream: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lIBZB8rX9E0
TomitankChess, the JavaScript chess engine discussed in the winning strategy: https://github.com/tomitank/tomitankChess

videos.title: VibeJam - AgentBattler.com
I Ran The Olympics For AI Coding
videos.description:
I'm building the Olympics for AI coding. Last weekend, the first event (Vibe Code Cup) challenged competitors to build a chess engine under 1MB in just 2 hours using AI tools. The winning submission took a genius shortcut: finding tomitankChess on GitHub (a 2900+ ELO JavaScript engine by Tamás Kuzmics, built over 9 years) and adapting it in a single prompt. This video breaks down why the edge in AI coding isn't the tools - it's the human judgment behind them.

🏆 Sign up for the next AI Coding Cup: https://aicodingcup.com
♟️ Continuous chess challenge: https://chessagents.ai
📦 tomitankChess repo: https://github.com/tomitank/tomitankChess

Topics covered: AI coding competition, vibe coding, Claude Code, chess engines, round-robin tournaments, harness engineering, sub-agent orchestration, feedback loops, prompt engineering strategies, neural network chess engines, Stockfish, and the future of competitive AI coding.

videos.title: Clawdbot x Meta Rayban x Amazon | From Photo to Purchase
videos.description:
first video/tutorial : https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3DBpfB0ao50
I finally successfully automated Amazon ordering using my Meta Ray-Ban glasses! In this video, I demonstrate a custom workflow where I take a photo of a product (peppermint gum) via WhatsApp, send it to my AI agent "Jinx," and have it automatically add the item to my Amazon cart.

Timestamps
00:00:00 - Demo: Scanning gum with glasses
00:00:50 - AI agent identifies the product
00:01:03 - Activating ClawdBot Chrome Extension
00:01:21 - Gum added to Amazon cart
00:01:50 - Local Mac vs AWS VPS
00:02:30 - No SDK needed: WhatsApp method
00:02:44 - Tutorial: ClawdBot Extension setup
00:03:20 - NPM install and loading extension
00:04:10 - Remote gateway via Cloudflare Tunnels
00:04:50 - Security risks and sandboxing
00:05:40 - Hardware costs: Mac Mini vs VPS

To bypass Amazon’s aggressive bot detection, I used the ClawdBot Chrome extension to control an authenticated browser session. I’ll walk you through the technical setup, including installing the extension via NPM, connecting my local machine to an AWS VPS using Cloudflare Tunnels (or Ngrok), and the trade-offs between running a Mac Mini 24/7 versus a cloud server.
Keywords: Meta Ray-Ban Smart Glasses, Amazon Automation, ClawdBot, WhatsApp Bot, Web Scraping, AWS VPS, Cloudflare Tunnels, Chrome Extension, AI Agent, Jinx.

videos.title: riverside tech magic episode   jan 08, 2026 arash's studio
Why I Think Skills Will Replace 80% of MCPs
videos.description:
In Part 3 of this research series, I double down on my controversial conclusion: Anthropic Skills can replace 80% of Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. Despite skepticism from the community, my latest benchmarks prove that replacing MCPs with Skills results in massive context savings without sacrificing performance.

I put this theory to the test by running a "Wordle Bench" comparing the Playwright MCP against Sawyer’s Dev Browser Skill. We also live-code a custom Exa API skill to see if we can replicate MCP functionality in under 10 minutes. The results show a 10x reduction in token usage and faster execution times, challenging the current consensus on how AI agents should interact with tools.

videos.title: Claude/Agent Skills Cut My Context Window by 98%
videos.description:
I benchmarked Playwright MCP, Claude Skills, the Claude Chrome Extension, and CLI alternatives on my Wordle Bench to find out which is fastest, cheapest, and least bloated.

Skills use progressive loading (90 tokens vs 7,000), but MCPs have no cold start. There are real trade-offs either way — and Anthropic says both still matter.

⏱️ TIMESTAMPS:

🎯 KEY TAKEAWAYS
→ Skills = less context bloat (90 tokens vs 7,000)
→ MCPs = no cold start, server runs immediately
→ Chrome Extension = expensive but handles screenshots
→ CLI/Bash = minimal overhead, maximum control
→ MCPs are becoming APIs for agents, Skills teach what to do with them

🔗 LINKS & RESOURCES

videos.title: I Finally Shipped SpeakMCP v1 — Here's Everything It Does
videos.description:
I am incredibly excited to finally launch SpeakMCP v1! After six months of development, this fully open-source native desktop app is ready to change how you interact with AI. Think of it as a bridge between voice control and the Model Context Protocol (MCP).
SpeakMCP allows you to spawn AI agents to control your terminal, manage GitHub issues, dictate notes into Notion, and interact with any third-party service—all with your voice or keyboard. It runs on Windows, Mac, and Linux, and supports local models like LlamaCPP or cloud providers like Groq and OpenAI. Whether you want to translate speech in real-time or run 5 coding agents in parallel, this tool puts completely general agents at your fingertips.

videos.title: Desktop Commander: An MCP Server for AI PC Control.
videos.description:
I'm putting Desktop Commander, an MCP (Model-Context-Protocol) server, to the test in this video. I've been searching for a tool that allows an AI to properly interact with my operating system, including clicking buttons and navigating the GUI. While Desktop Commander doesn't quite do that, it offers some powerful features like enhanced terminal commands, in-memory code execution, and instant data analysis.
Join me as I explore its capabilities, from interacting with running processes to its code editing and audit logging features. We'll see how it performs with different models like Grok 3 Mini and Sonnet 4 as I task it with analyzing a folder of CSV files on my desktop. Can this AI agent successfully navigate my file system and provide a summary, or will it fall short? Watch to find out my final thoughts on Desktop Commander and whether it's a viable tool for AI-powered OS automation.

videos.title: The v0 Challenge: I Built the World's Worst CAPTCHA
videos.description:
I took on the Vercel v0 challenge to build the most ridiculous and fun CAPTCHA possible. Inspired by the worst UX and bad UI memes, I used v0's generative AI to create a hilariously frustrating, two-step "Are you a human?" test.
First, you have to hold a button for three seconds as it violently shakes and tries to escape your cursor. Then, you face a number lottery where you can't type the code—you have to generate it. Can I even beat my own creation? Watch to see me struggle with this impossible CAPTCHA made with web development's newest AI tool.

videos.title: How I Use AI to Build My Open-Source MMO Game | Parallel remote agents
videos.description:
I put AI to the ultimate test: "vibe coding" 4 new features into my open-source Three.js MMO game in under two hours, all while live on stream. Using AI tools like Augment Code and its remote agents, I tasked the AI with implementing a death and respawn system, a working weapon and sword attachment, a force-field shield, and a berry harvesting mechanic.
Watch as I go from a bland world to a game with actual core mechanics, all by prompting and delegating to AI. We'll see the successes, the funny bugs (like a tiny sword!), and the incredible power of modern AI programming assistants. Can AI really build complex game features this fast?
This game is a cross-platform, browser-based MMO inspired by RuneScape, built with Three.js, JavaScript, and a serverless AWS backend.

Play the game: alpha.berigame.com

Check out the open-source code: https://github.com/aj47/berigame

videos.title: Unlock 4500+ MCPs to a lightweight client!
videos.description:
My Apify Affiliate link: https://www.apify.com?fpr=appricot

Discover and test mcp-client-cli, a lightweight Python command-line interface for interacting with MCPs and LLMs. Follow along as we explore its features, architecture (using SQLite for persistence!), and setup process. 

We then integrate mcp-client-cli with Apify to access a wide range of MCP tools like web scrapers (Instagram, Twitter, YouTube, Reddit) using a single API key. See live testing with different models like GPT-4o mini and GPT-4o, including debugging tool access issues and configuring Apify actors manually. videos.title: "General" AI Agents: Skylight (OS) vs Manus (Browser) vs Suna (Open Source)
videos.description:
In this video, I explore the world of AI agents designed to automate computer tasks. We take a deep dive into Skylight AI, a platform that promises agents capable of using Windows virtual machines just like a human. I test Skylight's capabilities by tasking it with scraping my top 5 YouTube Shorts, finding my channel, navigating the page, and creating an Excel spreadsheet with the results – all through direct OS and GUI interaction.
But how does it stack up against the competition? I run the exact same task on Manus AI and the newly released, open-source Suna AI. Watch as we compare their approaches (OS control vs. browser automation vs. scripting), speed, accuracy, cost, and overall user experience.
Join the discussion as we look at the strengths and weaknesses of each platform, the potential of OS-level automation vs. browser/terminal focus, and the current state of AI agents trying to become truly useful digital employees. Can these tools handle real-world tasks reliably yet? Let's find out!

Links:
OG stream: https://youtube.com/live/EIxRQm6kjak?feature=share
skylight: https://www.launchskylight.com 
manus: https://manus.im/
suna: https://www.suna.so/

videos.title: From Idea to Multiplayer Game in 10 minutes with WebSim
videos.description:
WebSim is an innovative tool that allows users to generate fully functional websites and complex simulations with simple prompts, without needing to code.

Key Takeaways:
- WebSim can create a wide range of projects, from simple websites to complex 3D simulations and games.
- Users can iterate on their projects using natural language prompts, refining and improving them quickly.
- WebSim utilizes PeerJS, a JavaScript library that enables peer-to-peer WebRTC connections, allowing for multiplayer functionality without requiring users to host their own servers.
- The tool supports collaborative development, letting users build upon and modify existing projects created by others.
- WebSim can generate both frontend and backend components, including API endpoints with dynamically generated responses.
- The WebSim community actively shares projects and guides through platforms like Discord and Reddit, fostering learning and creativity.
- The platform showcases impressive projects like procedurally generated worlds, AI agents, and Minecraft-like games, highlighting its versatility.
- WebSim's simulation capabilities include 3D simulations, particle simulations, and collaborative paint games.
- The speaker views WebSim as an exciting development at the intersection of software engineering and AI, demonstrating how these fields are accelerating each other's progress.
- WebSim can deploy servers and databases, simplifying the creation of online games and apps.
- The platform offers tools to refine and enhance existing projects, encouraging creativity and experimentation.

videos.title: Deepseek 7B vs. Wizard 33B: Coding AIs Showdown on HARD LEETCODE (GPT4 judge)
videos.description:
I compared two AI coding models, DeepSeek Coder 7B and Wizard Coder 33B, using a complex LeetCode question. I noted that despite Wizard Coder's larger size and slower speed, its coding capabilities seemed more advanced. I examined factors like time complexity and efficiency. Using GPT-4 for evaluation, I found that DeepSeek Coder was faster but less accurate, while Wizard Coder, albeit slower, provided a correct solution with some minor errors. My conclusion was that Wizard Coder, though more resource-heavy, outperformed in coding proficiency.

videos.title: Is this THE END for Junior Developers? - Sweep AI Wrote Perfect Code in 5 Minutes
videos.description:
PS. Excuse the clickbait title

🚀 While artificial intelligence is advancing rapidly, I think junior developers need not feel their jobs are threatened. It is an overstatement to suggest this technology can wholly replace entry-level roles. Rather, AI can streamline and enhance certain aspects of development, making it more accessible and enjoyable. 😃 There remains a great deal of complex work beyond the current capabilities of AI, providing ongoing opportunities for junior developers to apply their skills and grow professionally. 💪 Concerns about being displaced by technology are understandable, but may be somewhat premature at this stage. With the right guidance, AI can become a collaborative tool to complement human creativity rather than compete with it. 🤝🌟
